coredata.dc:title: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor constructed by encapsulating double carbon dots in zeolite imidazole frameworks for sensing Pb2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoqiong
Guo, Hao
Wu, Ning
Xu, Mengni
Zhang, Longwen
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission ratio fluorescent sensor (N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8) has been constructed successfully by introducing nitrogen-doped blue fluorescence carbon dots (N-CDs) and red fluorescence carbon dots (R-CDs) into the synthetic environment of ZIF-8 by a simple stirring method. The synthesized material not only inherits the excellent optical properties of N-CDs and R-CDs and the strong adsorption and accumulation performance of ZIF-8, but also shows strong fluorescence and structural stability in extreme environments. The presence of Pb2+ strongly causes the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs, but has almost no effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs. Therefore, a ratio fluorescence sensor is developed for the determination of Pb2+. Most importantly, the real-time fluorescence response shows that the composite material has a high selectivity and good sensitivity to Pb2+ determination, and the calculated LOD is 4.78 nM(S/N = 3) (0.05–50 μM), indicating that the material can be used as a potential fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zeolite imidazole frameworks
Fluorescence sensing
Lead ion
Green synthesis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Pb2+ in the actual water sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis route of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 and its application in sensing of Pb2+.
SEM images (a, b) and TEM images (c, d) of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8.

(a) XRD patterns of ZIF-8 and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8; (b) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, R-CDs and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8; (d) N2 absorption-desorption isotherms of pure ZIF-8 (black) and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 (red) collected at 77.35 K.

(a) Fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission spectra (red line) of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 in ethanol solution (The inset was the corresponding photographs of the material under natural light and 365 nm UV light); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, R-CDs, ZIF-8 and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 under excitation of 340 nm.
Fluorescence response of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 to different metal ion (λex = 340 nm).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 under different Pb2+ concentration; (b) the linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Pb2+ concentration (0.05-50 μM).
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Pb2+.
Determination of Pb2+ concentrations in actual water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual-emission fluorescence sensor constructed by encapsulating double carbon dots in zeolite imidazole frameworks for sensing Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE
Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, P R China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission ratio fluorescent sensor (N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8) has been constructed successfully by introducing nitrogen-doped blue fluorescence carbon dots (N-CDs) and red fluorescence carbon dots (R-CDs) into the synthetic environment of ZIF-8 by a simple stirring method. The synthesized material not only inherits the excellent optical properties of N-CDs and R-CDs and the strong adsorption and accumulation performance of ZIF-8, but also shows strong fluorescence and structural stability in extreme environments. The presence of Pb2+ strongly causes the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs, but has almost no effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs. Therefore, a ratio fluorescence sensor is developed for the determination of Pb2+. Most importantly, the real-time fluorescence response shows that the composite material has a high selectivity and good sensitivity to Pb2+ determination, and the calculated LOD is 4.78 nM(S/N = 3) (0.05–50 μM), indicating that the material can be used as a potential fluorescent sensor for Pb2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of urbanization and industrialization, heavy metal ions (Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+ and As3+) in the environment have caused serious harm to the ecological environment and human health because of their high toxicity and non-degradability [1–3]. Among all kinds of toxic heavy metal ions, Pb2+ is considered as one of the most dangerous pollutants, which widely exists in the environment closely related to us such as air, water, soil, food, and easily enters the human body through inhalation and ingestion [4,5]. Any form of Pb2+ accumulated in the human body is highly toxic, which will not only damage the nerve, hematopoiesis, digestion, immune system and kidney, but also cause a series of functional changes of cells, resulting in serious health risks, especially for children [6]. For this reason, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO) have set the limit of Pb2+ in drinking water as 72 nM (15 ppb) and 48 nM (10 ppb), respectively [7,8]. Up to now, many traditional analytical methods have been reported for measuring Pb2+ including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [11] and other methods. However, all these methods have some disadvantages of expensive instrument and equipment, complex sample pretreatment procedure, long sample analysis time and high cost [12]. Compared with these traditional detection methods, the fluorescence analysis method is favored by researchers because of its high sensitivity, good selectivity, simple operation and short response time [13]. Many fluorescent Pb2+ sensors have been designed using small molecules [14], DNA enzymes [15], polymers [16] and nanomaterials [17]. However, most of these Pb2+ sensors are based on the detection of single emission intensity, which may be susceptible to adverse effects of various environmental, concentration and instrument factors [18]. In contrast, the ratio fluorescence sensor uses the ratio of the emission intensities at two different wavelengths, and can be self-calibrated through dual-channel detection to eliminate environmental interference, so it can be designed as a sensor to accurately determine metal ions [19]. Accordingly, it is of great importance to study novel highly selective multifunctional fluorescent Pb2+ probes.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, were first discovered by Xu et al. during the synthesis, separation and purification process of arc discharged single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [20]. CDs have attracted attention from more and more researchers due to their unique optical and biochemical properties and potential application value in imaging [21], fluorescence sensing [22], photocatalysis [23], and drug delivery [24]. Compared with other carbon nanomaterials, CDs luminescence not only does not have the high toxicity of inorganic semiconductor quantum dots, but also overcomes the shortcomings of organic dyes luminescence unstable and easy photobleaching [25]. Therefore, they have become a kind of promising fluorescent materials for constructing various fluorescent probes and sensors for the detection of heavy metal ions, pollutant anions and biological small molecules, and achieved remarkable results [26–28]. In the past few decades, various methods used to synthesize CDs have been developed on account of their advanced characteristics [29]. Among them, the hydrothermal carbonization method utilizes various carbon precursors such as carbohydrates, ascorbic acid, and citric acid to synthesize CDs with the advantages of simplicity, environmental friendliness, safety and economy [30]. In recent years, the research of synthesizing fluorescent CDs with green natural substances as carbon sources has been upsurged. The synthesized carbon dots have the advantages of wide sources of raw materials, simple manufacture, low cost and environmental protection [31]. At present, the main materials reported are concentrated on vegetables, fruits and other plants. Most natural plant components contain sugars, proteins and other ingredients, which provide rich heterogeneous elements to form heteroatom-doping carbon dots during the synthesis process [32]. However, most of the CDs synthesized in the literature emit blue to green fluorescence. Therefore, it is very important and urgent to develop carbon dots of other colors. In nature, the chlorophyll in green leaves has strong near-infrared absorption and emission capabilities due to its porous structure [33]. Considering all these factors, in this present work, two kinds of CDs which respectively emit blue and red fluorescences under an ultraviolet lamp, were synthesized by using inorganic reagent and biomass raw material spinach, respectively.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are promising porous hybrid materials, which were composed of metal ions (clusters) and organic ligands through coordination bonds [34]. Because of ultra-high porosity, large specific surface area and uniform pore size distribution, they have been widely applied in various fields such as gas storage and separation, catalysis, drug delivery, biomedical imaging and chemical sensing [35–37]. Nevertheless, MOFs materials still have some shortcomings, such as poor thermal and chemical stability, which limits their further applications [38]. In recent years, with the discovery of new MOFs and the continuous deepening of related property and application research, MOFs-based composite materials that combine MOFs with other components have also been studied more and more extensively [39]. For example, MOFs-based composite materials with the excellent performance were prepared by encapsulating functionalized fluorescent materials such as quantum dots (QDs), fluorescent dyes and carbon dots (CDs) into the micro-pores of MOFs to combine the porous properties of MOFs and the fluorescent properties of fluorescent materials [40–42]. This is a good strategy for designing and synthesizing composite materials based on metal-organic frameworks. The resulted composite materials have diverse properties and can be widely used in chemical sensing, photocatalysis and drug delivery [43–45]. Among many reported MOFs, ZIF-8 has become the primary choice for synthesizing composite materials due to its unique advantages including strong stability and easy synthesis [46]. Ma et al. reported a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor (QDs/CDs@ZIF-8), based on the advantages of dual emission sensors in overcoming environmental interference and strong adsorption capacity of MOFs, the synthesized composite materials can be used for the detection of Cu2+ [47]. Inspired by this, we try to use CDs as fluorescent material to synthesize a novel luminescent composite material by a simple and effective method. It is expected that the composite material can maintain the excellent optical characteristics of CDs and can be used to specifically identify the target analytes.

To improve the selectivity and sensitivity of the measurement, nitrogen-doped carbon dots with blue fluorescence (N-CDs) were synthesized using citric acid and urea, and red fluorescence carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized with green raw material spinach (the synthesis diagram of N-CDs and R-CDs was shown in Fig. S1). The synthesized CDs with different optical properties were simultaneously introduced into the synthetic environment of ZIF-8 via a simple stirring method to form a novel dual-emission ratio fluorescent sensor (N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8) (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material not only has the excellent optical properties of N-CDs and R-CDs, but also has the strong adsorption and accumulation performance of ZIF-8, as well as strong fluorescence and structural stability in extreme environments. Based on the advantages the ratio sensor can effectively eliminate the interference caused by instrument instability and environmental changes, and selectively and sensitively detect Pb2+.

Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (297.49 g mol−1) and 2-methylimidazole (2-Hmin, 82.1 g mol−1) were provided from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Various metal nitrates including Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and Co2+ were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Citric acid (CA) and urea (UA) was received from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N,N’-dimethylformamide (DMF), N,N’-dimethylacetamide (DMA), formaldehyde (HCHO), acetonitrile (CH3CN), ethanol (EtOH), trichloromethane (CHCl3), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 1,4-dioxane, benzaldehyde, Methanol (MeOH), acetone, toluene, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and dialysis membranes (molecular weight cut off (MWCO:1000)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co. Fresh spinach was bought in the supermarket around the school to synthesize red fluorescent CDs. The chemicals used in this experiment were all analytical grades and could be used directly without further purification.

The crystal structure of the material was measured on a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with a Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by UV757CRT UV–vis spectrophotometer and FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured with KBr slices by using a Digilab FTS-3000 IR spectrometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) accessory was used to analyze the morphologies and composition of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were carried out on an FEI Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN instrument at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area, pore volume and pore sizes of the materials were measured through N2 adsorption and desorption experiment. The material was further freeze-dried on an FD-1−50 vacuum freeze-dryer.

Water-soluble N-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method [48]. Briefly, CA (1.26 g, 6 mmol) and UA (1.08 g, 18 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and stirred until the solid was completely dissolved, then, the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel and put into a thermostatic blast drying oven at 160 °C for 8 h until the reaction was over. After cooling to room temperature, the synthesized aqueous solution was first filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter head, and then dialyzed (MWCO: 1000) with deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the obtained solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

According to literature report [33], R-CDs were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method with spinach extract as a precursor. Firstly, fresh spinach was cut into small pieces, soaked in ethanol-containing water solution (the volume ratio of ethanol to water was 4:1) for 30 min, after filtering and collecting the clean spinach extract, the ethanol was removed by rotary evaporation at 60 °C, and then the green powder was obtained by freeze-drying. Dissolve 50 mg of the prepared precursor in 25 mL of ethanol to form a clear solution, and then transfer the obtained solution to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heat at 150 °C for 6 h. After the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature naturally, the obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous filter head and purified through a dialysis membrane for 24 h. Finally, the R-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

The N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composites were synthesized by a simple stirring method. Specifically, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1188 g) and 2-Hmin (0.5296 g) were respectively dissolved in 10 mL methanol (named as solution A and solution B respectively). The solution A was poured into the solution B rapidly under magnetic stirring. Then, 1 mL of N-CDs solution and 1 mL of R-CDs solution was added immediately, and the reaction mixture was continuously stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the composite was obtained after washed with methanol several times and dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 6 h.
For comparison, pure ZIF-8 was synthesized through the same preparation method without adding N-CDs and R-CDs.

Fixing the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the width of excitation and emission slit of 3 nm, a series of fluorescence measurement experiments were carried out on the FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer equipped with 1 × 1 cm2 quartz cell. Firstly, the fluorescence responses of the composite material in various organic solvents were assessed. Subsequently, the selectivity and specific recognition ability of the synthetic material were evaluated. Finally, the anti-interference ability of the material was further studied by employing the same concentrations of interfering ions as the detecting ion (10 mL, 1 × 10−2M).

The actual water samples were collected from laboratory tap water and Yellow River water (Lanzhou section). Prior to the experiment, the water samples was filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous filter head to remove insoluble impurities, and different concentrations of Pb2+ standard solutions were spiked into the obtained water sample, and then the solutions were respectively added to N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 ethanol solution, the exact amount of Pb2+ was obtained by the standard addition recovery method and the fitted linear equation to further evaluate the practical application value of the method.

A new composite material (N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8) was successfully synthesized by introducing blue fluorescent N-CDs and red fluorescent R-CDs into the growth environment of ZIF-8. By simulating the structure of ZIF-8 with diamond software, it was concluded that ZIF-8 was a kind of material combining large cavities and small apertures, in which each zinc ion coordinated with four nitrogen atoms belonging to imidazole ring (IM), and the interaction between zinc ion and 2-methylimidazole formed a three-dimensional network structure [49] (Fig. S2). SEM and TEM images were used to verify the morphology of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. 1 , the synthesized nano-probe had a rhombic dodecahedron structure with an average size of about 40 nm, which showed the same morphology as ZIF-8 crystal reported in the literature [46], indicating the successful synthesis of the nano-probe [50]. Fig. S3 was the TEM images of N-CDs and R-CDs, from which we can see that the synthesized N-CDs and R-CDs are small spheres with uniform particle size. By comparing powder XRD pattern of the synthesized ZIF-8 with the simulated data from a single crystal, it was found that most peaks from ZIF-8 were well preserved, indicating the successful synthesis of ZIF-8 ( Fig. 2 a). There were no significant changes in the powder XRD pattern were observed after introducing N-CDs and R-CDs into the ZIF-8, which indicated that the introduction of N-CDs and R-CDs had no effect on the crystalline integrity of ZIF-8 [51].

FT-IR analysis was carried out to verify the formation of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8. The FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 before and after introducing N-CDs and R-CDs were showed in Fig. 2b, there was a strong absorption peak at 3429 cm−1, which may be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. Two typical broad absorption peaks at about 3215 cm-1 and 1657 cm-1, corresponded to the stretching vibration peak and scissoring vibration peak of –NH2 or –N-H from N-CDs and imidazole, respectively. These results confirmed the existence of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CDs and R-CDs. The characteristic absorption peaks of N-CDs and R-CDs were observed in the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8, which confirmed the successful synthesis of the composite. As revealed in Fig. 2c, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a sharp absorption peak at 210 nm, accompanied by a wide absorption peak at about 340 nm and a small absorption peak at 680 nm. The sharp one could be considered as the typical π-π* transition of CC bonds, the broad one possibly belonged to the n-π* transition of CN bonds from aromatic structures [52,53]. The composite showed characteristic absorption peaks of N-CDs and R-CDs at the same time, which indicated the successful synthesis of the composite. N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a typical Type I BET isotherm with a specific surface area of about 176.32 m2 g-1. Compared with the pure ZIF-8 (249.02 m2 g-1), the BET surface area of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 reduced mainly due to the non-porous CDs entering the porous of ZIF-8 ( Fig. 2 d) [46].

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of solid-state N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composites were investigated at room temperature (Fig. S4). Considering the practical application, the fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized material in ethanol solution were further studied. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composite material had good dispersibility in an ethanol solution, which appeared clear and milky white under natural light (left inset of Fig. 3a), and could emit strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (right inset of Fig. 3a). Under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the composite material displayed two fluorescence emission peaks at 440 nm and 680 nm, respectively, which was consistent with the excitation and emission of the material in a solid state, and these two emission peaks could be respectively used as identification signal and reference signal to construct a ratio fluorescence sensor for the subsequent detection experiments. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, R-CDs, ZIF-8 and N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composites at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm were recorded in Fig. 3 b. The fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 showed two strong emission peaks at 440 nm and 680 nm, which respectively corresponded to the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and R-CDs at this excitation wavelength. Nevertheless, pure ZIF-8 had no obvious emission peak, which demonstrated that the blue fluorescence of the composite came from superposition of N-CDs and R-CDs instead of ZIF-8, and the fluorescence properties of N-CDs and R-CDs in ZIF-8 were still retained.

The luminescence and structural stability of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 in the extreme environments were further evaluated. It was found that the XRD pattern of the material showed almost no change after being treated in hot water at 373 K for 24 h, which indicated that the material had excellent structural stability (Fig. S5). At the same time, we also investigated the fluorescence properties of the material under different conditions, and found that the material had good acid resistance, salt resistance and storage stability. Even if it was stored in different pH values (4–11), salt solutions with a concentration of up to 1 M and room temperature for one week, its fluorescence intensity only changed slightly (Fig. S6-S8). Therefore, the material could be applied in diverse and complicated environments with a good environmental tolerance, which further revealed the great potential of the material in practical sensing applications.

In order to make the fluorescent probe more suitable to practical detection, the sensing performance of the material for different small organic molecules at room temperature were tested firstly (Fig. S9). After taking all the factors into consideration such as luminescence intensity, dispersibility, and environmental friendliness, the ethanol was finally chosen as the experimental solvent for further experiments. Subsequently, the selectivity of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material to different metal ions was further discussed. Some metal ions including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+ and Fe2+ (10 mL 10−2 M) were added into the ethanol solution of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material to record the changes of F440/F680, respectively. Fig. 4 showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio F440/F680 of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material had either no obvious change or slightly decreased with the addition of other metal ions except for Pb2+, which almost completely quenched the fluorescence of the material at 440 nm. The results showed that N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material had selective fluorescence response to the detection of Pb2+. As a control and comparative experiment, the fluorescence responses of the N-CDs, R-CDs and the mixtures of N-CDs and R-CDs to Pb2+ were investigated separately (Fig. S10). It is worth pointing out that the fluorescence intensity of the mixture of N-CDs and R-CDs decreased at 440 nm after adding of Pb2+, while that at 680 nm hardly changed, which was consistent with the response of individual CDs to Pb2+. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the material at 440 nm was derived from the quenching of N-CDs. Eventually, the anti-interference ability of the material was studied by testing the fluorescence response of Pb2+ after mixed with various coexisting metal ions to composite material (Fig. S11). The results showed that other metal ions had no or little influence on the detection of Pb2+, which indicated that the material had a high selectivity for the luminescence sensing of Pb2+.

In order to further evaluate the high sensitivity of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material to Pb2+, the fluorescence spectra of the composite material suspension in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+ were recorded in Fig. 5 a. It could be clearly seen that with the gradual increase of Pb2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 at 440 nm decreased accordingly, while that at 680 nm almost unchanged. As can be seen from Fig. 5b, there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F440/F680) of the material and the Pb2+ concentration (0.05−50 μM). According to the quenching slope (Ksv) of the fitting curve (Fig.S12) and standard deviation (Sb) of blank solution, the lowest detection limit (LOD = 3Sb/Ksv) was calculated to be 4.78 nM (S/N = 3), which was far lower than the safety limit of 72 nM and 48 nM recommended by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO) for Pb2+ in drinking water, respectively [7,8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ in the actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability and feasibility of this method, the content of Pb2+ in the water samples from laboratory and Yellow River was determined by using standard addition method. The determination results are given in Table 2 , it was found that the recoveries were 90.60 %–109.6 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 1.10 % ∼ 3.24 %, which indicated that the material can be successfully applied to the determination of Pb2+ in actual water samples.

The possible fluorescence sensing mechanism of Pb2+ to N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composite was investigated preliminarily (Fig. S13). After adding Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 440 nm was significantly weakened, and white flocculent precipitation appeared in the solution, which was likely caused by the coordination between Pb2+ and amino nitrogen atoms (Fig. S14). In order to explain the possible mechanism of the material's response to Pb2+ in detail, the influence of structural changes on fluorescence response was first explored. The XRD of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composite treated with Pb2+ solution was significantly different from the original XRD, and the peaks at about 7.5° and 10° disappeared, which indicated that the structure of the materials had been damaged after being treated with Pb2+ solution (Fig. S15). SEM and TEM images showed that the morphology of the N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material has changed significantly and the original dodecahedron structure has disappeared after being treated with Pb2+ solution (Fig. S16). From EDS mapping of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 after treated by Pb2+ solution, Pb2+ has been enriched on the composite and Zn2+ of ZIF-8 disappeared, the structure of the composite has also collapsed (Fig. S17). Fig. S18 showed the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 before and after treatment with Pb2+ aqueous solution. Through analysis, it could be seen that the stretching vibration peaks of NH at 3135 cm−1 and bending vibration peaks of NH at 1585 cm−1 significantly weakened or disappeared, indicating that Pb2+ coordinated with amino nitrogen of N-CDs, which led to the disappearance of vibration peaks. At the same time, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 composites before and after Pb2+ aqueous solution treatment were studied (Fig. S19). It is clearly showed that the characteristic absorption peak of the material at 340 nm was disappeared after Pb2+ treatment, accompanied by a new absorption peak at 253 nm, supporting that the nitrogen of N-CDs in the composite material participated in the complexation of Pb2+.

As a result, the effective fluorescence quenching of Pb2+ to N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8 material could result from the structure collapse of ZIF framework caused by Pb2+ coordination interaction with imidazole groups and fluorescence quenching of N-CDs induced by nitrogen-containing group coordination with Pb2+. At first Pb2+ was adsorbed and concentrated by the ZIF, and then concentrated Pb2+ led to decomposition of the ZIF framework and fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through coordination interaction. Obviously, the mechanism was different from fluorescence quenching of QDs/CDs@ZIF-8 caused by Cu2+, a similar work, where the fluorescence quenching of the QDs could be attributed to the strong binding of Cu2+ onto the surface of the QDs cores, resulting in the replacement of surface Cd2+ ions by Cu2+ ions, thus forming low soluble CuTe particles onto the surface of QDs [47].

In summary, N-CDs and R-CDs were introduced into the synthetic environment of ZIF-8 by a simple stirring method to prepare a novel ratio fluorescence probe (N-CDs/R-CDs@ZIF-8) for Pb2+ ion determination. The obtained composite material not only had the advantages of ratio fluorescence probe (eliminating background interference), but also could accumulate the analyte through the strong adsorption capacity of ZIF and improved the detection sensitivity. The composite material showed strong luminescence and structural stability under extreme environments, and could be applied to sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ with a wide linear range of 0.05−50 μM, and low LOD of 4.78 nM. The synthetic method of the probe was simple and cheap, and had potential value for detecting Pb2+ concentration in the environment sample.

Xiaoqiong Wang: Writing - review & editing. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Ning Wu: Formal analysis, Software. Mengni Xu: Investigation, Resources. Longwen Zhang: Project administration. Wu Yang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are very grateful to National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024) and Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126218.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A case study of heavy metal pollution in water of Bone River by artisanal small-scale gold mine activities in eastern part of Gorontalo, Indonesia
Water
